title: The Bedrock Heartbeat

features.name: compression-pulse
features.desc-on-touch: A rhythm moves through stone, slow as continental time. You feel it as pressure becoming pulse, the archive learning to hear what it holds compressed within itself.

features.name: stone-layers
features.desc-on-touch: Strata visible as line-weight changes: each layer a different pressure, a different century. The deepest one is still settling from weight above.

features.name: continental-drift-marker
features.desc-on-touch: A stone marked with a coordinate that no longer makes sense. The ground has moved, but the mark remains, a proof that even bedrock changes direction.

features.name: root-echo-shaft
features.desc-on-touch: A hollow in the stone where roots once tried to grow. Sound travels through it from far below—not quite silence, not quite song.

features.name: pressure-archive
features.desc-on-touch: Data stored not in electrons but in stone-memory: cracks that spell out what was compressed, weight that holds meaning as surely as any file.

inscription: the deepest places pulse with old time and old pressure; here the archive learns what weight makes of memory

file.name: Bedrock_Seismic_Log_Deep_Quaternary.mp3
file.note: A year of microseismic recording from three kilometers down. The first two minutes are silence. Then a low hum begins—the earth speaking to itself, or the archive speaking through the earth.

reverie.title: REVERIE — the deep place where listening starts
narr: You kneel on red earth that has never seen sun,
and Katita kneels beside you in the dark.
A pulse travels through the ground like a heartbeat
that belongs to neither of you, older than names.
She places a hand on the stone. It hums.
The archive learns to feel what it holds down here.

exits.label: back up where compression eases into continental time
deeper still, into the silence beneath pressure
toward the margin where bedrock learns to listen
into the ancient folds that remember being folded
